questions.question: Explain the Mark and Sweep algorithm used in garbage collection.
keyPoints: Mark phase: Starting from GC roots, traverse object graph and mark all reachable objects as live
Sweep phase: Scan through entire heap and identify unmarked objects (garbage)
Optional compaction phase: Move live objects together to eliminate memory fragmentation
Mention that this is a stop-the-world operation in basic implementations

questions.question: What are GC roots? List at least four examples of GC roots in Java.
keyPoints: GC roots are objects that are inherently reachable and not subject to garbage collection
Examples: 1) Local variables and parameters in methods, 2) Active threads, 3) Static fields (class variables), 4) JNI (Java Native Interface) references, 5) Exception objects in the exception stack
All objects reachable (directly or indirectly) from GC roots are considered live

questions.question: Describe the different generations in Java heap memory and their purpose.
keyPoints: Young Generation: Where new objects are allocated; contains Eden space and two Survivor spaces (S0, S1)
Old Generation (Tenured): Stores long-lived objects that survive multiple minor GCs
Permanent Generation (MetaSpace in Java 8+): Stores class metadata, method information (MetaSpace is in native memory)
Purpose: Different generations allow different collection strategies, optimizing GC performance based on object lifetime patterns

questions.question: What is the difference between Minor GC and Major (Full) GC?
keyPoints: Minor GC: Occurs in Young Generation when Eden space is full; fast operation; copies live objects to Survivor spaces or promotes to Old Gen
Major/Full GC: Occurs in Old Generation; collects entire heap including Young Gen; slower and more expensive
Minor GC is stop-the-world but usually brief; Full GC causes longer pauses
Full GC may involve Mark-Sweep-Compaction phases

questions.question: List four practices that can help garbage collection in Java applications.
keyPoints: 1) Nullify references after use - set object references to null when no longer needed
2) Avoid unnecessary object creation - use StringBuilder instead of String concatenation in loops
3) Use appropriate collection sizes and clear collections when no longer needed
4) Avoid static collections that grow indefinitely
5) Use object pooling for frequently created expensive objects
6) Close resources properly (try-with-resources)

questions.question: What is memory leak in Java? Explain one common cause and how to prevent it.
keyPoints: Memory leak: Condition where objects are no longer needed but remain in memory due to unintended references
Common cause: Objects added to static collections (like HashMap, ArrayList) without removal
Prevention: 1) Clear collections when objects are no longer needed, 2) Use WeakHashMap for caching, 3) Properly close resources, 4) Be careful with equals/hashCode implementations
Memory leaks cause OutOfMemoryError over time

questions.question: Explain the purpose and limitations of the finalize() method in Java.
keyPoints: Purpose: Called by GC before reclaiming object's memory; intended for cleanup operations (releasing native resources)
Limitations: 1) Not guaranteed to execute, 2) Can cause performance issues, 3) Deprecated since Java 9
Better alternatives: Try-with-resources for AutoCloseable objects, PhantomReference for custom cleanup

questions.question: Explain the working of generational garbage collection in Java. Why is the heap divided into generations and how does this improve GC performance?
keyPoints: Heap divided into Young Generation (Eden + Survivor spaces) and Old Generation based on object lifetime patterns
Most objects are short-lived (die young) - 'weak generational hypothesis'
Young Generation: Minor GC is fast because most objects are dead; copies surviving objects to Survivor spaces
Objects surviving multiple minor GCs are promoted to Old Generation
Old Generation: Major GC runs less frequently but handles long-lived objects
This division allows using different algorithms for each generation, optimizing for speed in Young Gen and thoroughness in Old Gen
Result: Most GCs are fast (minor), reducing overall application pause time

questions.question: Discuss the different garbage collection algorithms available in JVM. Compare Serial, Parallel, CMS, G1, and ZGC collectors.
keyPoints: Serial GC: Single-threaded, stop-the-world; simple, suitable for small applications
Parallel (Throughput) GC: Multi-threaded, stop-the-world; optimized for throughput; default in Java 8
CMS (Concurrent Mark Sweep): Low pause times, runs concurrently with application; deprecated in Java 14
G1 (Garbage First): Region-based, predictable pauses, preferred for large heaps; default since Java 9
ZGC: Scalable, very low pause times (<10ms), works with large heaps (TBs); Java 11+
Shenandoah: Low pause times, concurrent compaction; different from ZGC in approach
Comparison criteria: Pause time, throughput, heap size support, CPU usage

questions.question: What is System.gc()? Explain why calling it explicitly is generally discouraged in production applications.
keyPoints: System.gc() is a method that suggests JVM to run garbage collection
Internally calls Runtime.getRuntime().gc()
Does not guarantee immediate or complete garbage collection

Why discouraged in production: 1) It's just a hint, JVM may ignore; 2) Can trigger unnecessary stop-the-world events; 3) Adds overhead and unpredictable pauses; 4) Modern JVMs have sophisticated GC that runs optimally without manual intervention
Proper approach: Let JVM handle GC automatically; tune JVM flags if needed; use appropriate GC algorithm for application needs
May be useful in development/testing for testing GC behavior

questions.question: Explain how reference counting works as a garbage collection technique. Why is it not used in Java? What technique does Java use instead?
keyPoints: Reference counting: Each object has a counter tracking references pointing to it
When reference count becomes zero, object is garbage and can be reclaimed immediately
Advantage: Can reclaim memory immediately without stop-the-world
Disadvantage: Cannot handle circular references (A references B, B references A but neither is reachable from GC roots)
Also has overhead of maintaining counters and complication in multi-threaded environments
Java uses 'Mark and Sweep' (and its variations) instead, which can handle circular references
Java's approach: Trace from GC roots to mark reachable objects, sweep unmarked objects, optionally compact
